critique.summary: The article discusses how young workers, exemplified by Jackson Curtis, are reconsidering their career paths due to concerns about artificial intelligence potentially automating their current jobs. Curtis, an insurance underwriting assistant, is shifting to a career as a firefighter to avoid the risks posed by AI to office jobs involving data entry.
mainPoints: Young workers are worried about AI replacing their jobs, especially in roles involving repetitive tasks like data entry.
Jackson Curtis, an insurance underwriting assistant, is changing careers to become a firefighter to avoid AI job displacement.
There is uncertainty about the long-term impact of AI on various professions.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about workers fearing AI job displacement are consistent with broader labor market trends and concerns reported by multiple reputable sources. The example of Jackson Curtis is a specific anecdote illustrating this trend, which is plausible and aligns with documented shifts in career planning due to AI. However, the article provides limited data or multiple perspectives on the overall impact of AI on employment, relying mainly on a single personal story.
considerations: The article does not provide statistical data or expert analysis on AI's impact on employment across industries.
It lacks perspectives from employers, economists, or AI experts to contextualize the fears of job displacement.
The article does not discuss potential new job opportunities created by AI or how workers might adapt through upskilling.
It does not address the timeline or likelihood of AI fully automating roles like insurance underwriting.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the impact of AI on job markets, particularly the viability of trades versus white-collar work. Commenters debate whether trades are a safe refuge from automation and the future demand for software engineering and domain expertise.

comments.author: stephen_cagle
comments.text:
I'm somewhat skeptical of this "enter the trades" movement. Actually, I am more skeptical of that statement than I am of LLM's replacing white collar work in general. I think parts of coding are being replaced quickly because they are the parts that don't require discernment. Trades likely contain just as many automatable and just as many discernment parts as white collar work. At this moment in history, the automatable parts are being automated in the knowledge based world. People think the physical world is somehow different, but with world models (along the full spectrum of what that means) the physical world will be just as trainable as the knowledge based world.

tldr; Just like knowledge work, most trade stuff is probably mostly repeated (i.e. very trainable) task with a small amount of taste and discernment applied. The repeated will be trainable, the discernment may be trainable. I don't think the physical world is necessarily any safer than the knowledge world.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view on automation across job types, recognizing complexity in both trades and knowledge work, aligning well with current AI capabilities.

comments.author: margorczynski
comments.text:
The "go into trades" thing has two major flaws:

1) The supply of work will skyrocket when everyone will flock there for work

2) Demand will plummet as the white collar people who bought these services will loose their jobs and income

And of course if robotics will get solved to an acceptable degree most of those jobs will also get mostly automated.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly highlights economic feedback loops and potential automation in trades, showing awareness of broader market dynamics.

comments.author: ramesh31
comments.text: I'll say invest totally in domain knowledge now. The value of knowing how to invert a binary tree from memory has dropped to approximately zero. Web development as we knew it for the past 20 years is completely dead as an entry level trade. The power is shifting to people with useful knowledge and expertise that isn't about twiddling bits.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the shift from rote technical skills to domain expertise, which aligns with trends in AI automating routine coding tasks.

comments.author: striped_hash
comments.text: I don’t think kids should be insulating from AI. The examples in this article suggest for example that some people are dropping out of college and going into trade schools. I get that society needs electricians and construction workers and new software graduates are finding it difficult to get jobs. But having had a moderately successful career building software, I tend to think there is a lot of scope for the $40 trillion white collar economy to be disrupted (re-imagined/made more efficient), so still see potential for software engineering demand to stay high over the next decade as the true ramifications of AI plays out. Am I biased/coping? Is this moving faster? Slower? - What should kids be aiming for according to you? Computer Scientist? Biologist? Finance? Construction?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about career guidance amid AI disruption but is somewhat speculative about the persistence of software engineering demand.

comments.author: jongjong
comments.text:
Disturbingly, AI is set to replace essentially any position that is useful, to the extent that it is useful and somehow some people still think they should adapt themselves to the system instead of working to adapt the system to them!

Basically all that would be left of desk jobs would be those which have unfair legal powers (including via licenses and credentials) or are pure accountability plays. Like politicians, lawyers, aircraft pilots, corporate accountants... And those jobs will suck because people will be accountable for work that is not their own.

These jobs won't require any skills because most people may be able to go through their entire career without doing any work. But they will get paid a lot just for having being selected for their position... While other people who may be more skilled than them might be broke and homeless.
comments.analysis: The comment is pessimistic and speculative, assuming a dystopian job market with questionable claims about skill requirements and pay.

comments.author: cpach
comments.analysis: Provides only a link without context or commentary, offering no substantive contribution to the discussion.
